The surgeon initiates a myomectomy procedure by creating a pneumoperitoneum through a supra-umbilical entry, approximately two inches above the umbilicus. The technique involves aqua dissection, where 300-400 ml of normal saline mixed with vasopressin is injected into the myometrium.

The surgeon identifies the left and right fallopian tubes to determine the incision site for a posterior fundal fibroid. A myoma screw is placed on the fundal area to lift the fibroid. Vasopressin is injected into the myometrium, avoiding direct injection into the fibroid or its capsule.

The surgeon injects a solution of 40 units of vasopressin diluted in 400 ml of normal saline into the myometrium, causing the uterus to appear white. The fibroid is visible, and the injection is made approximately half an inch into the myometrium.

The surgeon uses a 10 cc syringe to inject vasopressin into the myometrium, ensuring not to enter a venous sinus by aspirating first. After injecting 30 ml, the pulse remains stable at 93, indicating no direct absorption into the bloodstream. The blood pressure is monitored at 110/70, consistent with hypotensive anesthesia. As the injection progresses to 40 ml, blanching of the tissue becomes more prominent.

fifty ml gone pulse is remaining the stable same no drop in the pulse makes it comfortable for me to keep on injecting further very rarely patients may not tolerate vasopressin especially if the vasopressin is getting absorbed directly into the bloodstream or a venous sinus in that situation the first sign to come up is bradycardia so from ninety if it drops to seventy or from hundred it drops to eighty that means it is not to be injected any further After having injected thirty forty ml if there has been no drop that means it is unlikely to now develop a further aspiration and drop later on. So then it is absolutely safe to keep injecting further.

The surgeon observes prominent blanching of the myometrium after injecting 150 ml of vasopressin solution, indicating effective vasoconstriction. The uterus appears blanched, while the fundal and fibroid areas remain unaffected, highlighting the targeted blanching of the uterine tissue.

The surgeon observes the vaginal area for any leakage of saline after injecting 100 ml of the vasopressin-saline solution into the myometrium. The solution is held in place by vasopressin to minimize bleeding during the myomectomy. The uterus appears sufficiently blanched, indicating effective vasopressin action.
The surgeon prepares to withdraw the needle after aqua dissection, aiming to avoid using an energy source. An elliptical incision is planned on the uterus, anticipating some capsular reduction.

The surgeon makes an elliptical incision, causing saline to leak out without excessive bleeding. A bipolar device is used, controlled by a foot switch, to manage bleeding. The saline, guided by vasopressin, surrounds the fibroid, allowing easy identification of the dissection plane.

The surgeon continues the dissection, noting that the process is easier due to the previous injection of vasopressin, which causes blanching of the myometrium. There is visible oozing from the tissue, which typically stops once the myometrium retracts after the fibroid is removed.

The surgeon performs aqua dissection, allowing the tissue to separate automatically in the correct plane. Small fibroid seedlings are visible. A previously coagulated vessel is cut and coagulated again, marking the only use of an energy source during the procedure.

The surgeon extends the elliptical incision significantly to accommodate the removal of multiple small fibroid seedlings. The dissection requires patience due to the challenging scenario, but the fibroids are eventually extracted.

The surgeon identifies the endometrial cavity while dissecting around a large fibroid, noting the presence of multiple smaller seedling fibroids. The dissection plane is visible, although not as smooth as usual.

The surgeon attempts to grip multiple smaller fibroids simultaneously but decides to focus on separating the current fibroid using aqua dissection, which minimizes bleeding. The fibroid is easily separated, and the surgeon prepares for detailed dissection of remaining tissue.

Careful shaving is performed to avoid over-thinning the posterior wall, which could lead to complications if the patient conceives. The serosa has been opened on the other side, and debulking continues until the cavity is exposed.

The surgeon removes small pieces of tissue for frozen section analysis. The effect of vasopressin is visibly diminishing, and small fibroid seedlings remain. The surgeon decides to stop further shaving and prepares to close the surgical site.

The surgeon prepares to use a barb suture or Stratafix for a triple layer closure of the surgical opening, ensuring the needle and suture remain above the opened endometrial area without penetrating the endometrial cavity.

The surgeon positions one end of the suture outside the 5mm port to prevent entanglement. An upside-down needle is used near the endometrial cavity, avoiding excessive depth. The anterior wall of the cavity is visible, with the posterior wall cut through.

The surgeon uses a 24 by 24 taper cut needle to suture the tissue, ensuring the suture does not penetrate the endometrial cavity. The needle holder lifts the tissue, revealing small seedling fibroids near the endometrial area, which the surgeon carefully avoids.
The surgeon places the first layer of stitches using a barb suture to close the defect, ensuring the sutures do not penetrate the endometrial cavity.

The surgeon places three sutures on the posterior wall of the uterus for the initial layer closure. Without a manipulator, the surgeon uses an instrument to elevate the uterus, preparing to take a deeper suture bite with the cavity opened.
The surgeon manipulates the thread to position it precisely for the second layer of suturing, ensuring the needle does not penetrate deeply into the lower layer of the uterine wall.

The surgeon uses barb sutures to tighten the layer, ensuring the entire length of the suture holds the tissue securely, providing superior hemostatic effect. A deeper bite is taken in the tissue to enhance stability.

The surgeon places a suture at the site where the serosa has opened up, taking a deep bite without penetrating through the serosa. Suction is applied to clear the area, ensuring the suture holds the tissue securely.

The surgeon completes the placement of the second layer of sutures on the myometrial layer of the uterus, ensuring the closure does not penetrate deeper than the serosa. The sutures are tightened incrementally, with additional bites taken on the left half of the myometrium.

The surgeon takes one more suture bite to complete the third and superficial layer of closure on the uterine wall. The double layer closure is achieved, and the uppermost layer is stitched using a baseball stitch technique, with the needle passing from inside out.
The surgeon performs baseball suturing by turning the needle to stitch inside out, completing the closure of the uppermost layer.

The surgeon places three or four sutures using a scissor to tighten the closure, focusing on removing a particular seedling that could impede the healing process. Other seedlings on the lower side are left untouched.
